g.data-narration: This lesson covers using color information to segment images into meaningful regions.
Grayscale uses only intensity while color segmentation uses RGB channels for richer discrimination.
Color segmentation uses multiple channels like R, G, and B for richer discrimination between regions.
Common color spaces include RGB, HSV, L*a*b*, and YCrCb each with different properties.
RGB is an additive color model that is device-dependent and sensitive to illumination changes.
HSV separates hue for color type, saturation for purity, and value for brightness.
L*a*b* is perceptually uniform where distance approximates human color perception.
g.text: Perceptually uniform - distance approximates human perception

g.data-narration: The comparison table shows advantages, disadvantages, and best uses for each color space.
Color thresholding includes single channel, multi-channel AND, and vector-based spherical boundaries.
g.text: One channel only (e.g., R greater than 150)
Example: HSV for red - H: 0-10 deg, S: 50-100%, V: 50-100%

g.data-narration: Clustering methods like K-means, Fuzzy C-means, and Mean Shift group similar colored pixels.
K-means iteratively assigns pixels to nearest centroid and updates centroids until convergence.
Color distance metrics include Euclidean, Mahalanobis for correlated data, and L*a*b* perceptual.
g.text: d = sqrt(dR squared + dG squared + dB squared)
d = sqrt(dL squared + da squared + db squared)
- Euclidean: Simple but not perceptually uniform
- Mahalanobis: Accounts for correlation, better for elliptical clusters

g.data-narration: The implementation pipeline includes input, preprocessing, color conversion, segmentation, and postprocessing.
Color segmentation applies to medical imaging, autonomous vehicles, object recognition, and industrial quality control.
Challenges include illumination variations, shadows, highlights, and computational complexity.
g.text: Solutions: Color normalization, white balance, illumination-invariant features, hardware optimization

g.data-narration: Key takeaways include choosing the right color space, distance metrics, segmentation methods, and best practices.
g.text: HSV for hue-based, L*a*b* for perceptual, YCrCb for skin detection
Euclidean for simple cases, Mahalanobis for correlated data, L*a*b* for perceptual
K-means for clustering, thresholding for specific colors, region growing for homogeneity
Combine color with edges/texture, preprocess for illumination, postprocess for clean results

g.data-narration: You can now explain color advantages, compare color spaces, and apply distance metrics for segmentation.
g.text: Explain advantages of color over grayscale
Compare RGB, HSV, L*a*b* color spaces
Apply distance metrics for color similarity
